our_argument_summary: The P-wave shadow zone at 104-140 degrees from an earthquake epicenter was first explained by Oldham (1906) and refined by Gutenberg (1913) as proof of a spherical Earth with a liquid outer core. The shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers. Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating because the derivation requires spherical geometry.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists for WIN-064. The dome's raw text files (02-wins.txt through 07-ai-context.txt) contain zero mentions of P-waves, shadow zones, or seismic data. WIN-064 was added in V51.0 but has no corresponding discussion in the dome's source material that was captured in our raw-text archive.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No validation exists — neither hardcoded nor computed. The dome's monitoring infrastructure has no seismic domain.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None identified. The dome model provides no mechanism for how a flat disc could produce angular shadow zones consistent with spherical refraction through a liquid core.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review does not make specific code claims about WIN-064's monitoring, and correctly treats it as a 'Std Model Explains' case rather than a code analysis target.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Searched all raw-text files (02-wins.txt through 07-ai-context.txt) and found zero mentions of P-wave, shadow zone, or seismic monitoring. No domain in monitor.py handles seismic validation. WIN-064 appears only in V51.0 WIN list text, with no monitoring infrastructure.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The P-wave shadow zone is foundational standard seismology, first explained by Oldham (1906) and refined by Gutenberg (1913). The phenomenon and its angular range (104-140 deg) are standard physics derived from spherical wave propagation through concentric density layers. The dome model adopts the same observed phenomenon and claims it 'matches dome geometry' without providing an alternative derivation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The P-wave shadow zone was first identified and explained in 1906 (Oldham) and 1913 (Gutenberg). The dome model added this as WIN-064 in V51.0 (2026). This is retrodiction of a 120-year-old observation, not a prospective prediction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome model provides no derivation from dome geometry (disc_radius, firmament_height, etc.) that arrives at the 104-140 deg shadow zone range. The claim states the shadow zone 'matches dome geometry' but provides no formula, no ray-tracing through disc layers, and no mechanism for how a flat disc produces concentric angular shadow zones. The angular range is adopted directly from standard spherical seismology.

holes_found.description: Gutenberg 1913 DOI may be incorrect. Our review cites DOI 10.1007/BF01800425 for Gutenberg (1913). The 10.1007 prefix is Springer, but Gutenberg's 1913 work was published in Physikalische Zeitschrift (vol 14, pp 1217-1218), not a Springer journal. His more detailed 1914 paper appeared in Nachrichten der Königlichen Gesellschaft der Wissenschaften zu Göttingen. The DOI could not be verified through web searches and may resolve to a different Gutenberg paper or not at all.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify DOI 10.1007/BF01800425 actually resolves to the correct Gutenberg paper. If it doesn't, either find the correct DOI or cite without DOI as: Gutenberg, B. (1913), 'Über die Konstitution des Erdinnern, erschlossen aus Erdbebenbeobachtungen', Physikalische Zeitschrift, 14: 1217-1218. Wrong DOIs have been found before in this review (WIN-008, 009, 014) — pattern continues.

holes_found.description: Angular range inconsistency between WIN-064 and the Seismic Tomography kill-shot section. WIN-064 states '104°-140°' but the Seismic Tomography section in Part 4 (generate-html.js line 1181) says '140–103° from epicenter'. The range endpoints differ (103° vs 104°) and the order is reversed. Standard seismology sources cite either 103° or 104° as the inner boundary — both appear in the literature — but our review should be internally consistent.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one value (104° is more commonly cited in textbooks) and use it consistently in both the WIN-064 detail and the Seismic Tomography section. Also standardize the order (104°-140° is more conventional than reversed).

holes_found.description: Our detail_evidence is thin compared to the strength of this argument. We correctly note the shadow zone assumes spherical geometry, but we miss several powerful points: (1) A flat disc has no spherical core to refract waves around — there would be no angular shadow zone at all, just simple attenuation with distance. (2) S-waves are completely blocked beyond 104°, proving a LIQUID layer — the dome model has no liquid core concept. (3) PKP waves (P-waves refracted THROUGH the core) arrive beyond 140°, proving the core is bounded — again requiring spherical shell geometry. (4) Seismic tomography has imaged mantle plumes, subduction zones, and inner core anisotropy at resolution inconsistent with a flat disc.
holes_found.recommendation: Strengthen detail_evidence with at least the S-wave complete shadow (proving liquid core) and the absence of any dome mechanism for angular refraction patterns. These make the argument much harder for a defender to dismiss.

holes_found.description: The dome's raw-text files contain NO discussion of P-wave shadow zones — not in the wins list, predictions, model, or any other page. WIN-064 was added in V51.0 but our archive may be incomplete for V51 content (v51.0-2026-04-05 directory is empty). We should verify what the dome model actually claims about this WIN on their live site before asserting what their claim is.
holes_found.recommendation: On next poller run, ensure V51.0 content including WIN-064's full dome-side description is captured. If the dome model truly provides no discussion beyond 'matches dome geometry,' that itself is worth noting in our review — the claim has no supporting argument on the dome's own site.

stronger_arguments.description: The S-wave complete shadow beyond 104° is an even stronger argument than the P-wave shadow. S-waves cannot propagate through liquids at all. Their complete absence beyond 104° proves a liquid layer exists at the core-mantle boundary. The dome model has no concept of a liquid core, no mechanism for S-wave absorption, and no explanation for why S-waves vanish at a specific angular distance. This is not just 'standard model explains' — it's 'standard model required, dome model has no alternative.'
sources: Lehmann 1936 (discovered inner core from PKiKP arrivals)

stronger_arguments.description: Seismic tomography resolution argument: modern seismic tomography resolves Earth's interior in 3D at ~50 km resolution. The images show subducting slabs, mantle plumes, and lateral velocity variations all consistent with a spherical, layered planet. A flat disc would produce fundamentally different travel-time curves that cannot be reconciled with the observed wavefield. This is not a matter of interpretation — it's computational ray-tracing that either fits spherical geometry or doesn't.

kernel_of_truth.description: The P-wave shadow zone IS a genuinely important and well-documented phenomenon. The dome model correctly identifies it as significant physical evidence worth discussing. The angular range 104-140° is precisely measured and the refraction physics is solid. Any serious cosmological model must account for seismic observations.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Identifying a real phenomenon is Step 1 of the dome model's recurring pattern (name real phenomenon → claim it matches your model → provide no derivation). The shadow zone's very existence is DERIVED from spherical geometry. You cannot claim the shadow zone supports your flat-disc model when the shadow zone was discovered, explained, and measured using spherical-Earth assumptions that produce correct predictions. The dome model would need to show that a flat disc produces the same angular shadow pattern via a different mechanism — it doesn't even attempt this.

deeper_analysis: WIN-064 is a straightforward 'Std Model Explains' case, but it's an unusually strong one because the P-wave shadow zone is not merely explained by standard physics — it was the FOUNDATIONAL EVIDENCE that established Earth's internal structure. Oldham (1906) used seismic shadow patterns to first prove Earth has a core. Gutenberg (1913) used the shadow zone boundary to calculate the core-mantle boundary depth at 2,900 km (modern value: 2,891 km — remarkably close). The dome model's claim that this 'matches dome geometry' is particularly hollow because the dome model describes a flat disc with no internal layered structure, no liquid core, and no mechanism for angular wave refraction. A flat disc would produce simple distance-dependent attenuation, not the sharp angular cutoff observed at 104°. The sharp boundary requires a spherical density interface — exactly what a sphere with a liquid core provides. This WIN is also notable for having zero presence in the dome's own raw text — it appears to have been added as a number-padding exercise in V51.0 without any substantive theoretical backing on the dome site itself.

citations_verified: Oldham 1906 — DOI 10.1144/GSL.JGS.1906.062.01-04.21 VERIFIED. Resolves to 'The Constitution of the Interior of the Earth, as Revealed by Earthquakes' by R.D. Oldham, Quarterly Journal of the Geological Society, Vol 62, pp 456-475. Correct title, journal, and year.
citations_failed: Gutenberg 1913 — DOI 10.1007/BF01800425 UNVERIFIED. Could not confirm this DOI resolves to Gutenberg's 1913 work. The 10.1007 prefix is Springer; Gutenberg's 1913 paper was published in Physikalische Zeitschrift 14: 1217-1218, which may not have a Springer DOI. His 1914 paper in Nachrichten der Königlichen Gesellschaft der Wissenschaften zu Göttingen is sometimes conflated with the 1913 work. DOI may be wrong or may reference a different Gutenberg publication.
